issue.title: The Week AI Became Infrastructure
issue.dek: The useful story this week is not "more AI news." It is that AI is reorganizing into infrastructure layers: model portfolios, agent operating systems, distribution surfaces, compute supply chains, and power politics.

sections.title: Model News
sections.headline: Model News
sections.summary: New models released, with a focus on capability, pricing, and benchmarks.

side_panel.title: Gemini 3.1 Pro landed quietly
side_panel.text: Google released Gemini 3.1 Pro in preview on February 19 and pushed it into the Gemini API, AI Studio, Vertex AI, Gemini Enterprise, Gemini CLI, Android Studio, NotebookLM, and the Gemini app. Google reported a 77.1% verified ARC-AGI-2 score, making it a serious reasoning-model entry rather than a routine Gemini refresh.

side_panel.title: Codex got a frontier work model
side_panel.text: OpenAI introduced GPT-5.3-Codex as its most capable agentic coding model, saying it combines GPT-5.2-Codex coding strength with GPT-5.2-style reasoning and professional knowledge, runs 25% faster, and can handle long-running research, tool-use, and execution tasks.

side_panel.title: OpenAI put Codex Spark on Cerebras-speed silicon
side_panel.text: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark on Cerebras, targeting near-instant coding interactions at more than 1,000 tokens per second. Cerebras is not a normal NVIDIA-style GPU card; its wafer-scale processor keeps far more compute and memory on one giant chip, which makes the hook ultra-low-latency coding loops rather than long background agents.

sections.title: Agents
sections.headline: Agents
sections.summary: A standing section for agents, agentic workflows, agent OS ideas, orchestration, tools, roles, artifacts, validation, and practical automation.

side_panel.title: Claude Code got more room to work
side_panel.text: Anthropic doubled Claude Code five-hour rate limits for Pro, Max, Team, and seat-based Enterprise plans, removed peak-hour reductions for Pro and Max, and raised Claude Opus API limits. That turns compute capacity into a visible product feature for agent users.

side_panel.title: Codex moved beyond code-only chores
side_panel.text: OpenAI's Codex update framed it as a computer-side collaborator: it can use more tools and apps, work across development workflows, generate images, remember preferences, continue repetitive tasks, inspect multiple files and terminals, use SSH, and iterate in an integrated browser.

side_panel.title: Agent models are being treated as security-sensitive
side_panel.text: OpenAI's GPT-5.3-Codex system card says the model is being treated as high capability in cybersecurity under its Preparedness Framework. That is a signal that coding agents are moving from productivity toys into controlled professional infrastructure.

sections.title: Platform Plays
sections.headline: Platform Plays
sections.summary: News on Google, Microsoft, OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta, Perplexity, Chinese/opensource companies, and other platforms pushing AI into everyday work surfaces.

side_panel.title: Google Book turns Gemini into the operating layer
side_panel.text: Google's AI laptop push frames Google Book as a low-cost successor to Chromebook, built around Gemini Intelligence, Magic Pointer actions, and Android/Google app integration. The Google Books angle matters because Google can pair consumer devices and Workspace distribution with a deep corpus, making the push feel like a platform-and-data play instead of another chatbot launch.

side_panel.title: ChatGPT ads became buyable infrastructure
side_panel.text: OpenAI opened a beta self-serve Ads Manager for U.S. advertisers, added CPC bidding, expanded measurement with Conversions API and pixel-based tooling, and named agency and ad-tech partners including Dentsu, Omnicom, Publicis, WPP, Adobe, Criteo, Kargo, Pacvue, and StackAdapt.

side_panel.title: Google changed the search surface again
side_panel.text: Google updated AI Mode and AI Overviews with more direct links, article suggestions, website previews on desktop hover, and more personal/community perspectives. That makes AI answers less like a search results page and more like a guided answer surface.

side_panel.title: Gemini kept spreading through work surfaces
side_panel.text: Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro rollout matters because the model is not isolated in a chat app; it reaches API, Vertex, Gemini Enterprise, CLI, Android Studio, NotebookLM, and consumer Gemini surfaces. Distribution is part of the model launch.

side_panel.title: Creative platforms are becoming command centers
side_panel.text: The platform-play story is not only Google and OpenAI. Tools like Canva keep pulling AI into the normal creation workflow, which means employees may adopt AI first through the software they already open every day.

sections.title: AI Infrastructure
sections.headline: AI Infrastructure
sections.summary: Compute access, chips, power, memory, and data-center capacity became product strategy this week.

side_panel.title: Anthropic took the whole Colossus 1 lane
side_panel.text: Anthropic said it signed with SpaceX to use all compute capacity at Colossus 1, adding more than 300 megawatts and over 220,000 NVIDIA GPUs within the month. The company tied that new capacity directly to higher Claude Code, Claude Pro/Max, and Claude Opus API limits.

side_panel.title: Anthropic is stacking compute partners
side_panel.text: The SpaceX deal joins Anthropic's disclosed compute pipeline: up to 5 GW with Amazon, 5 GW with Google/Broadcom beginning in 2027, a Microsoft/NVIDIA partnership including $30 billion of Azure capacity, and a $50 billion U.S. infrastructure investment with Fluidstack.

side_panel.title: Space-based AI stopped sounding hypothetical
side_panel.text: The Google-SpaceX segment says Google wants launch capacity for radiation-resistant TPUs tied to Project Suncatcher, its space-based machine-learning initiative. The practical angle is simple: if AI compute is power-constrained on Earth, Google is exploring solar-powered orbital data centers and SpaceX is the cheapest highway to orbit.

sections.title: Power Moves
sections.headline: Power Moves
sections.summary: A standing section for founder drama, lawsuits, acquisitions, surprise partnerships, platform politics, and industry titan maneuvering.

side_panel.title: Musk versus Altman stayed strategically relevant
side_panel.text: The Musk-Altman legal fight is more than founder drama because it touches control, mission, commercialization, and who gets to define the rules of the leading AI labs. It belongs in the newsletter only when tied to vendor risk and market power.

side_panel.title: Anthropic and SpaceX made an unlikely alliance
side_panel.text: Musk's companies and Anthropic have not looked like natural allies, yet Anthropic now gets Colossus 1 capacity from SpaceX. That is the power move: compute scarcity can override public rivalries.

side_panel.title: OpenAI's countermove is developer loyalty
side_panel.text: Codex is becoming OpenAI's wedge into everyday technical work: app, CLI, IDE extension, browser, local workflows, and faster model lanes. That makes it harder for developers to judge vendors only by chat-model quality.

side_panel.title: ChatGPT ads changed the incentive picture
side_panel.text: OpenAI's self-serve ChatGPT ads, CPC bidding, and measurement stack mean assistant surfaces are becoming monetized distribution channels. Boards should watch whether answer quality, trust, and commercial placement stay clearly separated.

side_panel.title: Google and SpaceX point to the next infrastructure frontier
side_panel.text: The podcast notes that Google already owns a SpaceX stake, has Project Suncatcher in motion, and is reportedly discussing TPU launches with SpaceX. If this proceeds, AI infrastructure politics expands from chips and clouds to launch capacity, satellites, solar power, and orbital operations.

approved_review.audience_guidance: Write for executives: emphasize strategic implications, business risk, vendor/platform movement, budget/ROI, and decisions leaders should watch.
